speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is January 2, 1996. We're with survivor David Klebanow. Our interviewer is Stella Eliezrie. We're in Laguna Hills, California, USA. And our language is English. Very good. All right, Stella, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's January 2nd, 1996. My name is Stella S-T-E-L-L-A Eliezrie, E-L-I-E-Z-R-I-E, and I have the honor and privilege to interview today Dr. David Klebanow, K-L-E-B-A-N-O-W. We're in Laguna Hills, California, and the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And begin when you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's January 2nd, 1996. We're in Laguna, California. I have the honor to interview Dr. David Klebanow. Dr. Klebanow, good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And happy new year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Same to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Could you please tell me when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on November 1st, 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 88. Full 88. I'm in my 89th year of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a city, Barisaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- at that time, it was Russia, and it is still Russia. It is west-- or east, approximately 40, 50 miles east of Minsk, as the bigger city, and then somewhere 100 miles or maybe more west of Smolensk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's Jewish name was Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he known?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses. Moses, usually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a whole-- wholesalesman, what was considered, I think, in Barisaw, it was the only business on wholesale in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wheat and corn and-- and whatever it is, you know, all connected with food, [INAUDIBLE] and food. He with our other two brothers, it was so-called, all right-- three brothers managed this business and they were very wealthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were very wealthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was he? When he wasn't working, what kind of things did he like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my father? My father-- he-- he is a son of Hasidic-- Hasidic Lubavitch Jew, you know, person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name, I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father, your father's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: His father? David. I'm after my grandfather. My grandfather died before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say he was the son of a Hasidic Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hasidic Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he also a Hasidic Jew, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a religious Jew. Still, you know, he told me he was a Lubavitcher [? Hassad. ?] Although, he didn't have any beard and short hair. And-- but he was going, as I remember, to a Hasidic shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of customs, Lubavitcher customs, do you remember your father teaching you from his father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special-- special Hasidic-- in my early years, when I was six, seven years, eight years, I was going with him almost every day in the morning to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father put on one pair of tefillin or two pairs of tefillin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one, on one side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your mother. What was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was born in a city where only 20 Jewish families were living, in a city called Vyazma. You can compare to the United States, Plymouth, Massachusetts. It is a old, old Russian city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlindman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna or [? Channa. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was a Jewish family doing in a town like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was-- Jews were permitted, at that time, to come to this area under few conditions-- if he is a wholesale [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] businessman, if he's a lawyer, if he's a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was your-- was her father in then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My [? father ?] was a wholesale business in-- in-- we-- we-- the name was colonial products. But it is not colonial-- it was all possible, you know. But one of the brothers was every day at the railroad station where wagons were unloaded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was on the wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. Wheat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --could be from-- from Kiev. From-- different color rice. Rice was all right. Rice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all the other foodstuffs were coming mostly from Ukraine or from center-- center-- central Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- was your mother also-- also an Orthodox woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father, my grandfather-- grandfather on the mother's side was a-- basically, he was a simple worker, otherwise he wouldn't-- he was working in a shoe business. Otherwise, he wouldn't be allowed to-- to be in Vyasma. Synagogue in Vyasma, it is a 20 Jewish families. It was a small Jewish-- on the top of a old house. One small room, where once a week, the Jews would gather for prayers. During the week, no prayers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about brothers and sisters? Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does-- is she alive now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's one-- 12-- 14 months older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your household growing up. What-- what kind of home was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home-- at the beginning-- you must understand, at the-- at the beginning I was going to a cheder. It was somewhere about six years I was going to a cheder. And I was in cheder approximately two years or, say, so. And on-- after two years, I revolted. My mother was not there. My grandmother in Vyazma--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother in Vyazma was very sick with a pneumonia, and she went, you know, to Vyazma. Only my father was there at home. And I revolted, and I said I'm not going to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go, you know, to-- to go-- start to go-- thinking about going to a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight-- eight and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I revolted. It is the only time that my father came. He gave me a spanking. I went for one day to the cheder. And I came back, and I say, I'm not going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Year my grandfather came-- probably some-- somehow my father informed-- my grandmother came and took me away to Vyazma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to go away from Barisaw, from this-- from this, oh, you know, form of life. You know, he took to me to-- to Vyazma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- my mother was in Vyazma.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed for eight, nine months in Vyazma-- almost-- and I was making preparations to enter a Russian-- it is called gymnasia-- eight year, from 10 years admitted, to approximately to [? 80 ?] years, you know, age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a government-- government school, government school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews and non-Jews alike?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were very-- in this-- in this [INAUDIBLE]-- maybe in the-- in Vyazma, maybe three or four Jews were in a whole-- the school. When I came back to Barisaw, Barisaw had a very large such gymnasia, you know, which, in each class, three-- three-- three equal-- equal classes. And there, I took my test, and I was admitted to the gymnasium-- gymnasia in Barisaw. I was there only for one year. Here, it was the time-- it was already a revolution, or already time, [INAUDIBLE] beginning the German occupation but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1918. In 1918, in-- in-- the Germans had to-- went-- or went already back to Germany, it was already after the war, and approximately the time of-- of the war. And-- and-- and my parents were very much afraid if the Bolsheviks are coming, they will be killed, because they were the second-richest people in the-- in the city, and another had a factory of-- a big factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved, basically, to Kiev. Kiev is the capital of Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For one year, I was going to school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, again, became the-- the place became too hot for my parents. And we decided all to move out of Russia, and we moved and finally landed in a city called Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bialystok was Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystok was Poland. And here we stayed for approximately 20-some years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life in Bialystok as a young boy, as a school-- as a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started school. I started school, and I was [? cut out ?] in a private school which is considered inferior.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The best schools were the government schools, considered. The government schools were the best, and it was very hard for a Jew to enter such a government school. So Jewish-- Jewish-- at the beginning, Jews had to go to private schools. There were three private such Jewish gymnasia in-- in-- in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is called for the three teachers, the [? Zeligman, ?] [? Liebenhaft, ?] [? Iderich ?] and [? Dereginski, ?] that is the owners of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what street it was on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on-- on-- how it was? It is on the main-- we'll say on the main street of-- in Bialystok. I don't remember [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When you weren't in school, studying, what kind of thing would a young boy do in Bialystok for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very much-- I was very much involved in soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you good?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you play soccer with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- at that time, I was 12 years of age-- you know, with-- with-- from other schools, from-- we-- we played every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with Jews and non-Jews alike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews, together. Yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? Where did you continue going to school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in Bialystok, I was [? 12. ?] And I saw that our school did not give a-- a diploma to enter a university.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the school, and I worked with my friend together with a teacher, and we took the test to a Polish government school, and we were admitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the main school was in a small city called Drohiczyn. You want to spell?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: D-R-O-H-I-C-A-- C-Z-Y-N, on-- on the River Bug. And that was-- and we were in a-- among 200, 200 students or 300, we were 20 Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a university?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- no, that was high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: High school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I will not say whether it was anti-Semitism. Sure, you know, we were separate. We and the Poles were separated, but we didn't experience. As a matter of fact, my parents were, at that time, very poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happened because, you know, there was all the money. They kept the money in the-- in the rubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was completely devaluated, and from the richest family, poorest. [INAUDIBLE] money-- [INAUDIBLE] money, which don't have any value.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family take care-- how did your father take care of your mother and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, basically, my mother opened a small, we'll say, restaurant, only dinner-- dinner time. And it became very popular. She was basically a very excellent cook. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok, yes. And the-- and she was, [INAUDIBLE], you know, which was for a while. It was not very, we'll see, respected, you know, as far as my parents concerned. You know, I had problems with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you know, I-- I was assisting-- assisting a girl, you know, and the girl, we were-- we'll say, in love. But in the last moment, she said, you know, my parents were glad. I was already in medical school. I was already in my fifth year of medical school, and she said my parents were glad they saw you as a-- as a-- my husband, but your parents. They were-- they have a factory-- factory in Bialystok from making-- making wool-- woolen material, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: How you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- and I didn't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you tell her you had once come from a wealthy family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she believe you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, she believed, but at this time, you know, in Bialystok, she's poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. When did you go-- where did you end up going to university?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? I entered a-- I graduated from high school in, uh, '26 as number-one student. Straight A's. I applied to the University of Vilna, nevertheless, with straight A's, and I was not accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm Jew. There was, you know, a scandal. The dean was taking money, admitting Jews not with excellent--

speaker: interviewer
text: Grades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grades. And I was not admitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was-- came into the open, and the next dean was very strict. I-- we had-- we had to take examinations. I take the entrance examinations, and with my tests, my-- my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your scores?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from the school, you know, I was admitted in 1927 to the medical school in Vilna. There, you know, you didn't go first to pre-med. You would graduate medical school, and it is six years lasting medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose a school in Lithuania as opposed to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- Lithuania, it was at that time, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at that time Poland. It was the closest-- the closest to me where I lived in Bialystok, the closest was Vilna. I had relatives. I could live there, you know, somehow sustain. It was, you know, it was problem with sustain. I have relatives there. They helped me when I was there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was, at that time, the most respected, you know, profession for a Jewish-- for a Jewish young boy, you know, a young guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like now--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in certain ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now. I don't consider. I will not advise my-- I'm glad that my sons didn't go into medicine, you know. Medicine is now here in the United States, with malpractices business, it's in the dog house, especially my specialty, obstetrics and gynecology, which was before, very, very respected-- respected became one of the worst specialties.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents happy that you chose medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. They help me very much but how-- as much they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, they-- they had a small business, and they helped me with very little money. I had to-- to-- to be a teacher, you know, be tutoring-- tutoring to make-- to make my money in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tutor, what subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, not salary-- everything from [INAUDIBLE] the high school and the-- not good in mathematics, fine, not good in-- in Polish language, fine, not good in Latin language, fine-- everything, you know. Latin was one of the most important languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your medical school training.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- training. I was-- six year--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- why did you choose obstetrics?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother from Vyazma, too, [? won-- ?] went to-- entered the gymnasia and then, you know, was admitted to Moscow medical school. And he became a doctor, and-- and he became an obstetrician-gynecologist. And then somehow, influenced-- oh, my uncle is in obstetrics, let me go and be in obstetrics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that is number one, and then, you know, just simple life. Life dictated. I graduated. I had to go for one year to Polish Army as a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you, first, about your-- before we go to that period, I want to just ask you, you had told me that you used to go home to Bialystok and run-- were you involved in some kind of a Jewish organization during the time you were in medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was involved in all the--

words.text: I was involved in all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the-- all the years-- all the years I was involved. In Vilna, I was involved in-- in-- it was called, you know, the Union of Jewish Medical Students.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you say that? And what-- was it in Polish or in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish. No, in Polish it was-- it was [? Radovski ?] Club Medical. Something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of that organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polish didn't-- didn't accept us. And we had somehow had representation for the-- before the dean and-- and professors. So you have to have a representation. So we organize as a Jewish. And then maybe it was-- maybe the Poles-- the dean advised make-- make your own organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of the organization? Was it a social organization? Was it educational?

speaker: interviewee
text: Educational, in the first-- [? first of all. ?] I was the-- I was the librarian at the beginning, and I was buying books, medical books for everyone. How we've got money, we would organize balls. You know, balls. A ball, you know, a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dance, and [? dance ?] it was usually, the best, usually became the best usual dance evening in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jews and non-Jews invited to that dance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was basically not Jewish. We wouldn't come. [INAUDIBLE] they were completely-- completely away from-- the Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Separate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many didn't talk to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it-- did being Jewish, in any way, restrict your education in medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will not tell restrict. No, the professors were very-- very equal to all-- to all. But it was-- we were sitting on the left side in the-- left side in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they were sitting on the right side. They were-- they were 90 people. We were 25, 30, but that was how the division. We did-- didn't dare to go to their side, and they will not go to our side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me earlier about a story about going to see a rabbi. Could you tell me that story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Now, the first year of medicine is-- one of the main subjects is anatomy or histology, you know, microscopic anatomy. But for anatomy, you have to have cadavers to study, to-- to study all the organs and how it is built. The Polish students brought the conditions that they would not allow us to work on their Christian cadavers.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would not allow the Jewish students to work on Christian cadavers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Christian cadavers. So we had to get-- to get-- you know, to get-- when we went to Jewish hospital, they said, yes, from time to time, we have, you know, a poor man without a family died. Nobody-- nobody takes-- [INAUDIBLE] took care. We went-- but you see, the religious Jews would come right away and will take and give him a decent burial. So we went, you know, to-- to the Rabbi of Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Rabbi was Chaim Eizer Rodianski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have-- was he in a synagogue? He had his own synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had met him in his-- in his-- where he lived, where he was teaching. I wouldn't know. I presume so. That is on the-- on [? Villenska ?] Street, yeah, called [? Villenska ?] Street. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the chief rabbi of Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the main rabbi? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: The main rabbi, yes. He was the main rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your question to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came and asked him, Rabbi, help us, Jewish students to get-- to get a cadaver. He said, please, no, you must understand-- he was very human-- you must understand me. Said, I would like to help you as much as possible. But this is a Jewish law that if a Jew died, within 24 hours, he has to be interred, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I cannot help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came with you to see the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came with you to see the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in-- oh, three-- three, from the 20-something Jew-- Jews, we-- I was the leading. [? Kapinski ?] was with me. And-- and-- I forgot-- from a very famous Jewish-- Jewish-- from a Jewish family very famous in Vilna, she was the third one. I-- maybe I recall her name. You know, it is-- it is just-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me this story about the hand, about shaking hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after we finished our conversation, we said, rabbi, thank you very much. He turned to us [INAUDIBLE] stretched the hand, shake-- shook hands with me, shook hands with doctor-- doctor-- with-- at that time, [? Tom ?] [? Kapinski, ?] and they-- and the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She stretches her hand, and he makes 180 degree turn at the side. And [? Kapinski, ?] who knew more about religion, put her hand down, and we left, and he told her that a rabbi will never give a hand to a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the rabbi suggest then? What-- what were you going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't give us any suggestions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I made many suggestions. Nevertheless, maybe indirect, nevertheless, in Jewish hospital, one died. We can-- we-- we-- we got this-- this body, and we had a body to-- but we didn't have-- so we needed at least, four or five bodies, you know, but it is with only one body, so 20 some, one would section the arms, the other with the-- the-- the legs, and the other the bowels, and the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the body when you were finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished, you brought back, you know, brought back to a burial.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a Jewish burial?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a Jewish burial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the Polish army now. You had said that you joined the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- I was accepted. I was not accepted-- I was called to go for one year to the Polish army, after I graduated from medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you pleased to go to the army? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was, you know, [INAUDIBLE] pleased. I would-- I like to go straight, you know, in-- into my profession. But if I had to go for one year to the army, I went to the army. It was in-- in-- in-- in Warsaw. It was a special medical-- military medical school for professional doctor-- for professional military doctors, and-- and we were all a part of this for only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were called to duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you actually go into the army then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when in 1939 did you go into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean 1933.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was happier in the school, medical school, this military medical school, where we-- we had a-- from one side, simple medical-- simple military education. And the-- and the-- besides we had lectures on military medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the students were already-- we were-- Jews were already only around 10 in 120. Something-- we have to stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we're going to finish now tape one, and we'll continue.
